FeedItems.Summary:
A recap of the latest servicing updates for .NET and .NET Framework for July 2026.

On July 14, 2026, we released .NET 10.0.10, .NET 9.0.18, .NET 8.0.29 updates.

## Release Notes

* [.NET July 2026 security Updates - .NET 10.0.10, .NET 9.0.18, .NET 8.0.29](https://github.com/dotnet/core/issues/10474)
* [.NET 8.0.29 and .NET SDK 8.0.129](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/8.0/8.0.29/8.0.29.md) ([Download](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0))

Also announced at [.NET Updates in July 2026](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/dotnet-and-dotnet-framework-july-2026-servicing-updates/).

## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in ASP.NET Core SignalR (Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime). This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A denial of service vulnerability exists in ASP.NET Core SignalR in .NET 8, .NET 9, and .NET 10. Stateful reconnect can be leveraged by an attacker to deny service to other users. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/67787 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High - **Score:** 7.5 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H/E:U/RL:O/RC:C` - **Weakness:** CWE-770 (Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ### Mitigation Factors Your ASP.NET Core app is only affected if it uses SignalR with stateful reconnect enabled. ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10.0 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-x64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.5 | 10.0.6 ### .NET 9.0 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-x64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.14 | 9.0.15 ### .NET 8.0 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-musl-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.linux-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.osx-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.25 | 8.0.26 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable nuget package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.29 Runtime or .NET 8.0.129 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.18 Runtime or .NET 9.0.316 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.10 Runtime or .NET 10.0.302 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Microsoft disclaims all warranties, either express or implied, including the warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-56170]( https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-56170) ### Acknowledgements Ky0toFu ### Revisions V1.0 (July 14, 2026): Advisory published.

If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable nuget package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.29 Runtime or .NET 8.0.129 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.18 Runtime or .NET 9.0.316 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.10 Runtime or .NET 10.0.302 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in ASP.NET Core SignalR (Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime). This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A denial of service vulnerability exists in ASP.NET Core SignalR in .NET 8, .NET 9, and .NET 10. Stateful reconnect can be leveraged by an attacker to deny service to other users. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/67787 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High - **Score:** 7.5 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H/E:U/RL:O/RC:C` - **Weakness:** CWE-770 (Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ### Mitigation Factors Your ASP.NET Core app is only affected if it uses SignalR with stateful reconnect enabled. ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10.0 Package name | Affected version | ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in ASP.NET Core SignalR (Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime). This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A denial of service vulnerability exists in ASP.NET Core SignalR in .NET 8, .NET 9, and .NET 10. Stateful reconnect can be leveraged by an attacker to deny service to other users. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/67787 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High - **Score:** 7.5 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H/E:U/RL:O/RC:C` - **Weakness:** CWE-770 (Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ### Mitigation Factors Your ASP.NET Core app is only affected if it uses SignalR with stateful reconnect enabled. ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10.0 Package name | Affected version | If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable nuget package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.29 Runtime or .NET 8.0.129 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.18 Runtime or .NET 9.0.316 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.10 Runtime or .NET 10.0.302 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET SMTP client (System.Net.Mail). This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A spoofing vulnerability exists in the SMTP client implementation (System.Net.Mail) in .NET 8, .NET 9, and .NET 10, where an attacker can spoof messages during message routing. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/130716 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** Medium - **Score:** 6.5 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N` - **Weakness:** CWE-116 (Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ## Affected If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable nuget package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.29 Runtime or .NET 8.0.129 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.18 Runtime or .NET 9.0.316 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.10 Runtime or .NET 10.0.302 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Events.RelatedBody: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET SMTP client (System.Net.Mail). This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A spoofing vulnerability exists in the SMTP client implementation (System.Net.Mail) in .NET 8, .NET 9, and .NET 10, where an attacker can spoof messages during message routing. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/130716 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** Medium - **Score:** 6.5 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N` - **Weakness:** CWE-116 (Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10.0 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable nuget package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.29 Runtime or .NET 8.0.129 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.18 Runtime or .NET 9.0.316 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.10 Runtime or .NET 10.0.302 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET HTTP client (System.Net.Http). This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A denial of service vulnerability exists in .NET 8, .NET 9, and .NET 10 where an attacker can exploit the HTTP/2 protocol to cause an out-of-memory condition. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/130715 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High - **Score:** 7.5 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H` - **Weakness:** CWE-770 (Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10.0 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable nuget package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.29 Runtime or .NET 8.0.129 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.18 Runtime or .NET 9.0.316 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.10 Runtime or .NET 10.0.302 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET HTTP client (System.Net.Http). This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A denial of service vulnerability exists in .NET 8, .NET 9, and .NET 10 where an attacker can exploit the HTTP/2 protocol to cause an out-of-memory condition. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/130715 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High - **Score:** 7.5 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H` - **Weakness:** CWE-770 (Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10.0 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ## TL;DR
A source‑generated ordinary method that is **added in an EnC delta** (never present in the gen‑0 baseline assembly) can later fail to be mapped to the previous generation, crashing the delta emitter. When the method is subsequently **updated** (or deleted), `DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle` finds it neither in `EmitBaseline.MethodsAdded` (its tracking was dropped while the baseline was carried forward) nor in gen‑0 metadata (it was never there) → returns null.

This is a **distinct root cause** from the "intermediate empty update" desync tracked by  #84560 and **dotnet/roslyn#79898** (see *Relationship to #84560 and #79898* below). It reproduces on a single, ordinary, valid edit and does **not** require an empty/no‑change update.

## Symptom
On a normal Hot Reload edit, the update fails with:

```
System.InvalidOperationException: Unexpected value 'MauiApp13.MainPage.UpdateComponent()'
of type 'Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.CSharp.Symbols.SourceOrdinaryMethodSymbol+SourceOrdinaryMethodSymbolSimple'
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle(...) DefinitionMap.cs:line 194
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DefinitionMap.GetDeletedSynthesizedMethods(...) DefinitionMap.cs:line 751
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.<CreateDeletedMemberDefs>g__addDeletedClosureMethods|87_2(...) DeltaMetadataWriter.cs:line 609
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.<CreateDeletedMemberDefs>g__getDeletedMemberDefs|87_1(...) DeltaMetadataWriter.cs:line 604
   ...
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.CreateDeletedMemberDefs(...) DeltaMetadataWriter.cs:line 525
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.CSharp.Emit.EmitHelpers.EmitDifference(...)
   at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.EditAndContinue.EditSession.EmitSolutionUpdateAsync(...) EditSession.cs:line 1401
```

Surfaced to the user as `ENC1002: Cannot apply changes -- unexpected error`.

> Note: the `InvalidOperationException: Unexpected value ...` message comes from a **local build** of Roslyn that fails fast (`ExceptionUtilities.UnexpectedValue`) at `DefinitionMap.cs:194` instead of dereferencing null. In a stock/shipping build this same spot throws an opaque `NullReferenceException`. See the *Fail‑fast patch* note.

## Environment
- Compiler: Roslyn (local build; the fail‑fast guard is a local addition — see below). Reproduces on stock Roslyn as an NRE at the same location.
- Host: Visual Studio 18 (Canary), Managed Hot Reload / EnC (OOP `RemoteEditAndContinueService`).
- Target: .NET 11 preview, `net11.0-windows10.0.19041.0`.
- Generator: .NET MAUI XAML incremental Hot Reload generator (`Microsoft.Maui.Controls.SourceGen.XamlGenerator`), which emits a source‑generated `UpdateComponent()` partial method per Hot‑Reload‑enabled page. The method is **not** emitted in the baseline build (only on/after the first XAML edit), so it only ever exists in EnC deltas.

## Repro (conceptual)
1. Build & run a MAUI app whose page uses the XAML incremental Hot Reload generator. The baseline assembly does **not** contain `MainPage.UpdateComponent()`.
2. Make a XAML edit → apply. A delta **adds** `UpdateComponent()` (and, over subsequent edits, updates it — its body may contain lambdas/closures, e.g. event wiring / templates).
3. Continue editing across several generations. At some point a generation cannot map `UpdateComponent()` to the immediately preceding generation's symbol.
4. A later edit **updates** `UpdateComponent()`. Delta emit walks updated methods to reconcile their closures and calls `GetPreviousMethodHandle(UpdateComponent)` → crash.

The captured session log shows the failing update is an ordinary `ValidChanges` edit (no intermediate empty update, and with the #79898 empty‑update fix already applied).

## Root cause
`DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle` (`src/Compilers/Core/Portable/Emit/EditAndContinue/DefinitionMap.cs`) resolves the previous‑generation handle of `oldMethod` by:
1. looking it up in `Baseline.MethodsAdded` (methods added in a *preceding delta*), then
2. falling back to `PreviousSourceToMetadataSymbolMatcher.MapDefinition(...)` (the *gen‑0 metadata*).

For a delta‑added source‑generated method both can miss:
- It was never in gen‑0 metadata → step 2 returns null.
- Its `MethodsAdded` entry was **silently dropped** when the baseline was carried to a later generation.

The drop happens in `SymbolMatcher.MapBaselineToCompilation` → `MapDefinitions(baseline.MethodsAdded)` (`src/Compilers/Core/Portable/Emit/EditAndContinue/SymbolMatcher.cs`). `MapDefinitions<K,V>` re‑keys each entry via `MapDefinition(pair.Key)` and, when that returns null, **omits the entry** (by design, for deleted/uncompiled‑synthesized definitions):

```csharp
// SymbolMatcher.MapDefinitions<K,V>
var key = (K?)MapDefinition(pair.Key);
// Result may be null if the definition was deleted, or if the definition
// was synthesized (e.g.: an iterator type) and the method that generated
// the synthesized definition was unchanged and not recompiled.
if (key != null)
    result.Add(key, pair.Value);
```

A source‑generated **ordinary** method (`SourceOrdinaryMethodSymbolSimple`) can fall through this filter across generations. Once its `MethodsAdded` row is dropped, its handle is unrecoverable via the two lookups above, and any later update/delete of the method crashes the emitter.

## Relationship to dotnet/roslyn#79898 and #84560 
Same *symptom* (`GetPreviousMethodHandle` → null → crash) and same feature area, but a **different mechanism**:

| | #79898 (empty‑update desync) | This report (root cause #2) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | edit → **empty/no‑change** update → edit | a single ordinary `ValidChanges` edit |
| Mechanism | committed source‑generated docs advance past the emit baseline on `ModuleUpdateStatus.None` | `MethodsAdded` tracking for a delta‑added SG method is dropped while carrying the baseline forward |

## Suggested fix directions (for the Roslyn team)
- Preserve `MethodsAdded` tracking for a delta‑added definition whose metadata row still exists, even when `MapDefinition` returns null for a given generation (i.e., don't let a mapping miss discard a still‑live added row), **or**
- Recover the previous handle from the accumulated baseline metadata / `DeletedMembers` when `MethodsAdded` and gen‑0 both miss, rather than returning null.

A naive "skip the unmappable member" workaround in the delta writer is risky: if the row actually persists in accumulated metadata (tracking lost, not truly absent), skipping it can later produce a **duplicate** row on re‑add.

## Fail‑fast patch (context for the stack)
The local Roslyn build converts the three `Get*Handle` null derefs into descriptive fail‑fasts so the failure is diagnosable instead of an opaque NRE:

```csharp
// DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle / GetPreviousPropertyHandle / GetPreviousEventHandle
if (peMethod == null)
    throw ExceptionUtilities.UnexpectedValue(oldMethod);
```

This does not change behavior in a shipping build beyond the exception type/message.

[MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-rootcause2.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/30225601/MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-rootcause2.zip)

## Attached evidence (`MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-rootcause2.zip`)
- `crash-stack.txt` — full exception and stack.
- `Session.log` — EnC Hot Reload session log; the failing update is logged as `ValidChanges` (see the last `MauiApp13custom` session).
- `Analysis.log` — detailed per‑update analysis log.
- `MainPage.xaml.uc.xsg.cs` — the generated `UpdateComponent()` source (the ordinary method that fails to map), if present.

Events.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Hot Reload (EnC): NullReferenceException in `DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle` deleting a delta-added source-generated method (MAUI XAML SourceGen `UpdateComponent`)" (#84560) at dotnet/roslyn
Events.RelatedBody:
## Version Used

- Visual Studio 18.10 (Canary), 18.10.12018.7.
- **.NET MAUI built from the `feature/xaml-incremental-hotreload` branch** — PR [dotnet/maui#34338 — "[XAML] Incremental XAML Hot Reload (source-generated patch chains)"](https://github.com/dotnet/maui/pull/34338). This is the incremental XAML Hot Reload work that emits the `UpdateComponent()` patch-chain shown below. Targets `net11.0-windows10.0.19041.0` / `net11.0-android37.0`.
- XAML **SourceGen** hot reload enabled: `<MauiXamlHotReload>SourceGen</MauiXamlHotReload>`.
- Note: this private MAUI build was used to exercise the incremental XAML Hot Reload code path, but the failure itself is in Roslyn's EnC delta emit. It may therefore also reproduce with a released MAUI preview that has the SourceGen hot-reload path (possibly around preview 5 — unconfirmed).

## Symptom

During a Hot Reload session mixing XAML and C# edits, applying changes intermittently fails:

```
error ENC1002: Cannot apply changes -- unexpected error:
'System.NullReferenceException: Object reference not set to an instance of an object.'
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle(IMethodSymbolInternal oldMethod, IMethodSymbolInternal& peMethod)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.<CreateDeletedMemberDefs>g__getDeletedMemberDefs|87_1(...)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.<CreateDeletedMemberDefs>g__recurse|87_0(...)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.CreateDeletedMemberDefs(EmitContext, SymbolChanges)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.CommonPEModuleBuilder.CreateDeletedMemberDefinitions(DiagnosticBag)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.CSharp.MethodCompiler.CompileMethodBodies(...)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.CSharp.Emit.EmitHelpers.EmitDifference(...)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.EditAndContinue.EditSession.EmitSolutionUpdateAsync(...)
```

The session can't apply further updates until a rebuild.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a **.NET MAUI** app running against the `feature/xaml-incremental-hotreload` MAUI build (PR [dotnet/maui#34338](https://github.com/dotnet/maui/pull/34338)); ensure `<MauiXamlHotReload>SourceGen</MauiXamlHotReload>` (SourceGen path). *(May also reproduce on a released preview with the SourceGen hot-reload path.)*
2. Enable **Hot Reload on Save** in VS.
3. F5.
4. Make several **XAML** edits, applied both by saving and by the Hot Reload toolbar button.
5. Make several **C#** edits, again both ways.
6. Intermittently, an apply fails with the `ENC1002`/NRE above.

**Flaky** — no fixed sequence, but the ingredients are consistent: XAML SourceGen HR + interleaved XAML/C# edits + a mix of save- and button-triggered applies.

## Root cause (confirmed from EnC file-log capture)

The deleted member is a **source-generated method**, `MainPage.UpdateComponent()`, emitted by MAUI's `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.SourceGen.XamlGenerator` into a dedicated hot-reload document `MainPage.xaml.uc.xsg.g.cs`. It replays accumulated XAML property diffs as versioned blocks:

```csharp
// MainPage.xaml.uc.xsg.g.cs  (Microsoft.Maui.Controls.SourceGen.XamlGenerator)
public partial class MainPage
{
    internal void UpdateComponent()
    {
        if (__version == 0) { /* [5] Text = "Click me!=)" */ __version = 1; }
        if (__version == 1) { /* [5] Text = "Click me!"   */ __version = 2; }
        return;
    }
}
```

Each XAML hot-reload edit grows `UpdateComponent()` with another version block; periodically the generator regenerates the document **without** `UpdateComponent()` at all — which EnC sees as a **delete** of that method.

The crash is in [`DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle`](https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/blob/main/src/Compilers/Core/Portable/Emit/EditAndContinue/DefinitionMap.cs), reached from `DeltaMetadataWriter.CreateDeletedMemberDefs`:

```csharp
peMethod = (IMethodSymbolInternal?)PreviousSourceToMetadataSymbolMatcher.MapDefinition(oldMethodDef)?.GetInternalSymbol();
Debug.Assert(peMethod != null);                         // release: no-op
Debug.Assert(peMethod.MetadataName == oldMethod.MetadataName);
return (MethodDefinitionHandle)MetadataTokens.EntityHandle(peMethod.MetadataToken);  // NRE
```

`peMethod` is null because the deleted method resolves to **neither** `Baseline.MethodsAdded` **nor** the initial (gen-0) metadata. The captured deltas prove why:

- The **baseline** `MauiApp13.dll` does **not** contain `UpdateComponent` → it was never in gen-0 metadata.
- The **delta** metadata blobs (`1.meta`, `2.meta`, `6.meta`) **do** contain it → it was added in **delta** generations.

So `UpdateComponent` only ever lived in deltas. When it is later deleted, the lookup should succeed via `Baseline.MethodsAdded`, but that entry has been lost across generations: in [`SymbolMatcher.MapDefinitions`](https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/blob/main/src/Compilers/Core/Portable/Emit/EditAndContinue/SymbolMatcher.cs), any `MethodsAdded` entry whose `MapDefinition` returns null is silently dropped when the baseline is carried to the next generation:

```csharp
var key = (K?)MapDefinition(pair.Key);
// Result may be null if the definition was deleted, or if the definition
// was synthesized ... and the method that generated [it] was unchanged and not recompiled.
if (key != null) { result.Add(key, pair.Value); }
```

A source-generated method that isn't recompiled in an intervening generation drops out of `MethodsAdded`; a later delete then falls through to the gen-0 metadata lookup (which fails) → the unguarded null deref.

A second, independent way to reach the same crash (matches #79898's "intermediate empty update" repro): `DebuggingSession.EmitSolutionUpdateAsync`, on `ModuleUpdateStatus.None`, commits a freshly source-generator-refreshed solution (`LastCommittedSolution.CommitChanges`), which can advance committed generated content past the last emitted `EmitBaseline`.

## Captured evidence (attached zip)

[MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-repro.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/30178565/MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-repro.zip)

Full EnC file logs for a reproducing session are attached (`MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-repro.zip`), trimmed to a single TFM (`net11.0-windows10.0.19041.0`). Layout:

- **`MauiApp13.dll` / `.pdb`** — the **baseline** the session started from (generation 0).
- **`N.il` / `N.meta` / `N.pdb`** — the emitted **delta** for update *N* (metadata / IL / PDB).
- **`obj_Debug_..._Microsoft.Maui.Controls.SourceGen.XamlGenerator\MainPage.xaml[.uc].xsg.<update>.<generation>.{old,new}.cs`** — the **source-generated documents** diffed on each update. `xsg` = the `InitializeComponent` file; **`uc.xsg` = the `UpdateComponent()` hot-reload file** (the method in the crash).

The three files that prove the root cause:

1. **`MauiApp13.dll`** (baseline) — does **not** contain `UpdateComponent` → it was never in gen-0 metadata.
2. **`1.meta`, `2.meta`, `6.meta`** (deltas) — **do** contain `UpdateComponent` → it was added purely in **delta** generations.
3. **`...\MainPage.xaml.uc.xsg.8.3.old.cs`** contains `UpdateComponent()`, and its `.new` counterpart is absent → the update that **deletes** it.

So a source-generated method that only ever existed in deltas is later deleted; by then its `Baseline.MethodsAdded` entry has been dropped across generations, so `GetPreviousMethodHandle` can resolve it via neither `MethodsAdded` nor gen-0 metadata → the `NullReferenceException`.

## Related

- #79898 — same NRE/stack; its repro (intermediate empty update) corresponds to the `ModuleUpdateStatus.None` path above. This report adds a second, independent root cause — loss of `MethodsAdded` tracking for a delta-added source-generated method — with a concrete MAUI SourceGen repro and captured deltas proving the method lived only in deltas.

## Notes for a fix (non-prescriptive)

- The unguarded `Debug.Assert(peMethod != null)` in `GetPreviousMethodHandle` (and the property/event equivalents) should fail with a diagnosable error rather than a bare `NullReferenceException` in release.
- The underlying issue is that a delta-added source-generated member can lose its `MethodsAdded` handle across generations, so its later deletion can't be emitted.
- May also be worth looping in the .NET MAUI SourceGen team (@StephaneDelcroix), since the `UpdateComponent()` add/delete churn across HR generations is the trigger.

## Version Used

- Visual Studio 18.10 (Canary), 18.10.12018.7.
- **.NET MAUI built from the `feature/xaml-incremental-hotreload` branch** — PR [dotnet/maui#34338 — "[XAML] Incremental XAML Hot Reload (source-generated patch chains)"](https://github.com/dotnet/maui/pull/34338). This is the incremental XAML Hot Reload work that emits the `UpdateComponent()` patch-chain shown below. Targets `net11.0-windows10.0.19041.0` / `net11.0-android37.0`.
- XAML **SourceGen** hot reload enabled: `<MauiXamlHotReload>SourceGen</MauiXamlHotReload>`.
- Note: this private MAUI build was used to exercise the incremental XAML Hot Reload code path, but the failure itself is in Roslyn's EnC delta emit. It may therefore also reproduce with a released MAUI preview that has the SourceGen hot-reload path (possibly around preview 5 — unconfirmed).

## Captured evidence (attached zip)

[MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-repro.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/30178565/MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-repro.zip)

Full EnC file logs for a reproducing session are attached (`MauiApp13-EnC-UpdateComponent-repro.zip`), trimmed to a single TFM (`net11.0-windows10.0.19041.0`). Layout:

- **`MauiApp13.dll` / `.pdb`** — the **baseline** the session started from (generation 0).
- **`N.il` / `N.meta` / `N.pdb`** — the emitted **delta** for update *N* (metadata / IL / PDB).
- **`obj_Debug_..._Microsoft.Maui.Controls.SourceGen.XamlGenerator\MainPage.xaml[.uc].xsg.<update>.<generation>.{old,new}.cs`** — the **source-generated documents** diffed on each update. `xsg` = the `InitializeComponent` file; **`uc.xsg` = the `UpdateComponent()` hot-reload file** (the method in the crash).

The three files that prove the root cause:

1. **`MauiApp13.dll`** (baseline) — does **not** contain `UpdateComponent` → it was never in gen-0 metadata.
2. **`1.meta`, `2.meta`, `6.meta`** (deltas) — **do** contain `UpdateComponent` → it was added purely in **delta** generations.
3. **`...\MainPage.xaml.uc.xsg.8.3.old.cs`** contains `UpdateComponent()`, and its `.new` counterpart is absent → the update that **deletes** it.

So a source-generated method that only ever existed in deltas is later deleted; by then its `Baseline.MethodsAdded` entry has been dropped across generations, so `GetPreviousMethodHandle` can resolve it via neither `MethodsAdded` nor gen-0 metadata → the `NullReferenceException`.

This report adds a second, independent root cause — loss of `MethodsAdded` tracking for a delta-added source-generated method — with a concrete MAUI SourceGen repro and captured deltas proving the method lived only in deltas.

## Notes for a fix (non-prescriptive)

- The unguarded `Debug.Assert(peMethod != null)` in `GetPreviousMethodHandle` (and the property/event equivalents) should fail with a diagnosable error rather than a bare `NullReferenceException` in release.
- The underlying issue is that a delta-added source-generated member can lose its `MethodsAdded` handle across generations, so its later deletion can't be emitted.
- May also be worth looping in the .NET MAUI SourceGen team (@StephaneDelcroix), since the `UpdateComponent()` add/delete churn across HR generations is the trigger.

## Version Used

- Visual Studio 18.10 (Canary), 18.10.12018.7.
- **.NET MAUI built from the `feature/xaml-incremental-hotreload` branch** — PR [dotnet/maui#34338 — "[XAML] Incremental XAML Hot Reload (source-generated patch chains)"](https://github.com/dotnet/maui/pull/34338). This is the incremental XAML Hot Reload work that emits the `UpdateComponent()` patch-chain shown below. Targets `net11.0-windows10.0.19041.0` / `net11.0-android37.0`.
- XAML **SourceGen** hot reload enabled: `<MauiXamlHotReload>SourceGen</MauiXamlHotReload>`.
- Note: this private MAUI build was used to exercise the incremental XAML Hot Reload code path, but the failure itself is in Roslyn's EnC delta emit. It may therefore also reproduce with a released MAUI preview that has the SourceGen hot-reload path (possibly around preview 5 — unconfirmed).

## Symptom

During a Hot Reload session mixing XAML and C# edits, applying changes intermittently fails:

```
error ENC1002: Cannot apply changes -- unexpected error:
'System.NullReferenceException: Object reference not set to an instance of an object.'
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle(IMethodSymbolInternal oldMethod, IMethodSymbolInternal& peMethod)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.<CreateDeletedMemberDefs>g__getDeletedMemberDefs|87_1(...)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.<CreateDeletedMemberDefs>g__recurse|87_0(...)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.DeltaMetadataWriter.CreateDeletedMemberDefs(EmitContext, SymbolChanges)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Emit.CommonPEModuleBuilder.CreateDeletedMemberDefinitions(DiagnosticBag)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.CSharp.MethodCompiler.CompileMethodBodies(...)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.CSharp.Emit.EmitHelpers.EmitDifference(...)
  at Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.EditAndContinue.EditSession.EmitSolutionUpdateAsync(...)
```

The session can't apply further updates until a rebuild.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a **.NET MAUI** app running against the `feature/xaml-incremental-hotreload` MAUI build (PR [dotnet/maui#34338](https://github.com/dotnet/maui/pull/34338)); ensure `<MauiXamlHotReload>SourceGen</MauiXamlHotReload>` (SourceGen path). *(May also reproduce on a released preview with the SourceGen hot-reload path.)*
2. Enable **Hot Reload on Save** in VS.
3. F5.
4. Make several **XAML** edits, applied both by saving and by the Hot Reload toolbar button.
5. Make several **C#** edits, again both ways.
6. Intermittently, an apply fails with the `ENC1002`/NRE above.

**Flaky** — no fixed sequence, but the ingredients are consistent: XAML SourceGen HR + interleaved XAML/C# edits + a mix of save- and button-triggered applies.

## Root cause (confirmed from EnC file-log capture)

The deleted member is a **source-generated method**, `MainPage.UpdateComponent()`, emitted by MAUI's `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.SourceGen.XamlGenerator` into a dedicated hot-reload document `MainPage.xaml.uc.xsg.g.cs`. It replays accumulated XAML property diffs as versioned blocks:

```csharp
// MainPage.xaml.uc.xsg.g.cs  (Microsoft.Maui.Controls.SourceGen.XamlGenerator)
public partial class MainPage
{
    internal void UpdateComponent()
    {
        if (__version == 0) { /* [5] Text = "Click me!=)" */ __version = 1; }
        if (__version == 1) { /* [5] Text = "Click me!"   */ __version = 2; }
        return;
    }
}
```

Each XAML hot-reload edit grows `UpdateComponent()` with another version block; periodically the generator regenerates the document **without** `UpdateComponent()` at all — which EnC sees as a **delete** of that method.

The crash is in [`DefinitionMap.GetPreviousMethodHandle`](https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/blob/main/src/Compilers/Core/Portable/Emit/EditAndContinue/DefinitionMap.cs), reached from `DeltaMetadataWriter.CreateDeletedMemberDefs`:

```csharp
peMethod = (IMethodSymbolInternal?)PreviousSourceToMetadataSymbolMatcher.MapDefinition(oldMethodDef)?.GetInternalSymbol();
Debug.Assert(peMethod != null);                         // release: no-op
Debug.Assert(peMethod.MetadataName == oldMethod.MetadataName);
return (MethodDefinitionHandle)MetadataTokens.EntityHandle(peMethod.MetadataToken);  // NRE
```

`peMethod` is null because the deleted method resolves to **neither** `Baseline.MethodsAdded` **nor** the initial (gen-0) metadata. The captured deltas prove why:

- The **baseline** `MauiApp13.dll` does **not** contain `UpdateComponent` → it was never in gen-0 metadata.
- The **delta** metadata blobs (`1.meta`, `2.meta`, `6.meta`) **do** contain it → it was added in **delta** generations.

So `UpdateComponent` only ever lived in deltas. When it is later deleted, the lookup should succeed via `Baseline.MethodsAdded`, but that entry has been lost across generations: in [`SymbolMatcher.MapDefinitions`](https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/blob/main/src/Compilers/Core/Portable/Emit/EditAndContinue/SymbolMatcher.cs), any `MethodsAdded` entry whose `MapDefinition` returns null is silently dropped when the baseline is carried to the next generation:

```csharp
var key = (K?)MapDefinition(pair.Key);
// Result may be null if the definition was deleted, or if the definition
// was synthesized ... and the method that generated [it] was unchanged and not recompiled.
if (key != null) { result.Add(key, pair.Value); }
```

A source-generated method that isn't recompiled in an intervening generation drops out of `MethodsAdded`; a later delete then falls through to the gen-0 metadata lookup (which fails) → the unguarded null deref.

A second, independent way to reach the same crash (matches #79898's "intermediate empty update" repro): `DebuggingSession.EmitSolutionUpdateAsync`, on `ModuleUpdateStatus.None`, commits a freshly source-generator-refreshed solution (`LastCommittedSolution.CommitChanges`), which can advance committed generated content past the last emitted `EmitBaseline`.

- May also be worth looping in the .NET MAUI SourceGen team (@StephaneDelcroix), since the `UpdateComponent()` add/delete churn across HR generations is the trigger.

### Summary

Blazor WebAssembly diagnostics feature switches (`MetricsSupport`, `EventSourceSupport`, `HttpActivityPropagationSupport`) never turn on by default because their enabling condition reads a property name that is never defined.

In [`src/BlazorWasmSdk/Targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly.Current.props`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/main/src/BlazorWasmSdk/Targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly.Current.props#L47-L50) the default is set on `BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled`, but the three feature switches gate on `BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled` (note `Wasm` vs `WebAssembly`):

```xml
<BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled Condition="'$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == ''">true</BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled>
<MetricsSupport Condition="'$(MetricsSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</MetricsSupport>
<EventSourceSupport Condition="'$(EventSourceSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</EventSourceSupport>
<HttpActivityPropagationSupport Condition="'$(HttpActivityPropagationSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</HttpActivityPropagationSupport>
```

`BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled` is never assigned anywhere, so it always evaluates to empty, the `== 'true'` gate is always false, and the three switches stay at the low-level WASM runtime default of `false`. The result is that `Meter`, `Activity`/distributed tracing, and `EventSource` infrastructure is trimmed to no-ops in the browser — exactly the diagnostics that the block's own comment says should be on by default for Aspire/OpenTelemetry scenarios.

The opt-out documented in the comment (`BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled`) also does not match the property that is actually defined (`BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled`).

### Steps to reproduce

Create (or use) a Blazor WebAssembly project that does not set the feature switches explicitly, and evaluate the resolved properties:

```
dotnet build YourWasmApp.csproj -getProperty:BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled -getProperty:MetricsSupport -getProperty:EventSourceSupport -getProperty:HttpActivityPropagationSupport
```

### Expected

With `BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled` defaulting to `true`, all three feature switches default to `true`:

```
BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled = true
MetricsSupport                      = true
EventSourceSupport                  = true
HttpActivityPropagationSupport      = true
```

### Actual

```
BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled = true
MetricsSupport                      = false
EventSourceSupport                  = false
HttpActivityPropagationSupport      = false
```

Verified on SDK `11.0.100-preview.7.26356.101`.

### Suggested fix

Make the three conditions (and the opt-out named in the comment) reference the property that is actually defined, `BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled`:

```xml
<BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled Condition="'$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == ''">true</BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled>
<MetricsSupport Condition="'$(MetricsSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</MetricsSupport>
<EventSourceSupport Condition="'$(EventSourceSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</EventSourceSupport>
<HttpActivityPropagationSupport Condition="'$(HttpActivityPropagationSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</HttpActivityPropagationSupport>
```

Also update the comment so the documented opt-out property matches (`BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled`).

Applying this change locally to the installed SDK's copy of the props file flips all three switches to `true` by default, and opting out via `-p:BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled=false` (or setting the individual switches) continues to work.

Blazor WebAssembly diagnostics feature switches (`MetricsSupport`, `EventSourceSupport`, `HttpActivityPropagationSupport`) never turn on by default because their enabling condition reads a property name that is never defined.

In [`src/BlazorWasmSdk/Targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly.Current.props`](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/blob/main/src/BlazorWasmSdk/Targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly.Current.props#L47-L50) the default is set on `BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled`, but the three feature switches gate on `BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled` (note `Wasm` vs `WebAssembly`):

```xml
<BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled Condition="'$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == ''">true</BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled>
<MetricsSupport Condition="'$(MetricsSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</MetricsSupport>
<EventSourceSupport Condition="'$(EventSourceSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</EventSourceSupport>
<HttpActivityPropagationSupport Condition="'$(HttpActivityPropagationSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</HttpActivityPropagationSupport>
```

`BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled` is never assigned anywhere, so it always evaluates to empty, the `== 'true'` gate is always false, and the three switches stay at the low-level WASM runtime default of `false`. The result is that `Meter`, `Activity`/distributed tracing, and `EventSource` infrastructure is trimmed to no-ops in the browser — exactly the diagnostics that the block's own comment says should be on by default for Aspire/OpenTelemetry scenarios.

The opt-out documented in the comment (`BlazorWasmDiagnosticsEnabled`) also does not match the property that is actually defined (`BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled`).

### Steps to reproduce

Create (or use) a Blazor WebAssembly project that does not set the feature switches explicitly, and evaluate the resolved properties:

```
dotnet build YourWasmApp.csproj -getProperty:BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled -getProperty:MetricsSupport -getProperty:EventSourceSupport -getProperty:HttpActivityPropagationSupport
```

### Expected

With `BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled` defaulting to `true`, all three feature switches default to `true`:

```
BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled = true
MetricsSupport                      = true
EventSourceSupport                  = true
HttpActivityPropagationSupport      = true
```

### Actual

```
BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled = true
MetricsSupport                      = false
EventSourceSupport                  = false
HttpActivityPropagationSupport      = false
```

Verified on SDK `11.0.100-preview.7.26356.101`.

### Suggested fix

Make the three conditions (and the opt-out named in the comment) reference the property that is actually defined, `BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled`:

```xml
<BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled Condition="'$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == ''">true</BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled>
<MetricsSupport Condition="'$(MetricsSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</MetricsSupport>
<EventSourceSupport Condition="'$(EventSourceSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</EventSourceSupport>
<HttpActivityPropagationSupport Condition="'$(HttpActivityPropagationSupport)' == '' and '$(BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled)' == 'true'">true</HttpActivityPropagationSupport>
```

Also update the comment so the documented opt-out property matches (`BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled`).

Applying this change locally to the installed SDK's copy of the props file flips all three switches to `true` by default, and opting out via `-p:BlazorWebAssemblyDiagnosticsEnabled=false` (or setting the individual switches) continues to work.

Issues.Title: [release/3.1] Reset current_no_gc_region_info after leaving no gc region implicitly
Issues.Body:
Backport https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/73602.

## Customer Impact
Customers reported occasional `System.InvalidOperationException` with message `The NoGCRegion mode was already in progress` when calling `GC.TryStartNoGCRegion`. This is preventing them from using this optimization and impacting their cold start latencies.

## Testing
Customers have tried the fix on .NET 6 overnight and reported the exception never appears again. Meanwhile, there is an unpatched machine and observed 72 exceptions of the same type in the same time interval, proving this does fix the bug.

## Risk
Low. This only impact customer using `TryStartNoGCRegion`, and this is the right thing to do.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "[release/3.1] Reset current_no_gc_region_info after leaving no gc region implicitly" (#28265)

Issues.Title: Migrate FabricBot Tasks to Config-as-Code
Issues.Body:
**TL;DR;** Requesting to add [FabricBot](https://eng.ms/docs/products/1es-data-insights/merlinbot/extensions/fabricbot_overview) configuration associated with your repository to `.github/fabricbot.json`.

**Context**

FabricBot is now a [config-as-code-only](https://eng.ms/docs/products/1es-data-insights/merlinbot/extensions/bot-config-as-code) platform. As a result, while you can still use the [FabricBot Configuration Portal](https://portal.fabricbot.ms/bot/?repo=dotnet/coreclr) to modify your FabricBot configuration, you can no longer save the changes. The only way to save changes to your configuration at the moment is to _export configuration_ from the portal and upload the exported configuration to `.github/fabricbot.json` in your repository. In this pull request, we are adding your FabricBot configuration to your repository at `.github/fabricbot.json` so that you can make changes to it going forward.

While the [FabricBot Configuration Portal](https://portal.fabricbot.ms/bot/?repo=dotnet/coreclr) is the *only way* to modify your FabricBot configuration at the moment, we have a feature on our backlog to publish the JSON schema defining the structure of the FabricBot configuration file. With the JSON schema, you can (1) use a plaintext editor of your choice to modify the FabricBot configuration file and use the schema to validate the file after editing or (2) [configure](https://code.visualstudio.com/Docs/languages/json#_json-schemas-and-settings) VS Code to use the schema when editing FabricBot configuration file to take advantage of convenience features such as automatic code completion and field description on mouseover.

[*Pull Request Create*](https://eng.ms/docs/products/1es-data-insights/merlinbot/extensions/prcreate), a [MerlinBot](https://https://eng.ms/docs/products/1es-data-insights/merlinbot) Extension, was used to automatically create this pull request. If you have any questions or concerns with this pull request, please contact [MerlinBot Expert DRI](mailto:merlinbotdri@microsoft.com).
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Migrate FabricBot Tasks to Config-as-Code" (#28256)

Issues.Title: Migrate FabricBot Tasks to Config-as-Code
Issues.Body:
**TL;DR;** Requesting to add [FabricBot](https://eng.ms/docs/products/1es-data-insights/merlinbot/extensions/fabricbot_overview) configuration associated with your repository to `.github/fabricbot.json`.

**Context**

FabricBot is now a [config-as-code-only](https://eng.ms/docs/products/1es-data-insights/merlinbot/extensions/bot-config-as-code) platform. As a result, while you can still use the [FabricBot Configuration Portal](https://portal.fabricbot.ms/bot/?repo=dotnet/cli) to modify your FabricBot configuration, you can no longer save the changes. The only way to save changes to your configuration at the moment is to _export configuration_ from the portal and upload the exported configuration to `.github/fabricbot.json` in your repository. In this pull request, we are adding your FabricBot configuration to your repository at `.github/fabricbot.json` so that you can make changes to it going forward.

While the [FabricBot Configuration Portal](https://portal.fabricbot.ms/bot/?repo=dotnet/cli) is the *only way* to modify your FabricBot configuration at the moment, we have a feature on our backlog to publish the JSON schema defining the structure of the FabricBot configuration file. With the JSON schema, you can (1) use a plaintext editor of your choice to modify the FabricBot configuration file and use the schema to validate the file after editing or (2) [configure](https://code.visualstudio.com/Docs/languages/json#_json-schemas-and-settings) VS Code to use the schema when editing FabricBot configuration file to take advantage of convenience features such as automatic code completion and field description on mouseover.

[*Pull Request Create*](https://eng.ms/docs/products/1es-data-insights/merlinbot/extensions/prcreate), a [MerlinBot](https://https://eng.ms/docs/products/1es-data-insights/merlinbot) Extension, was used to automatically create this pull request. If you have any questions or concerns with this pull request, please contact [MerlinBot Expert DRI](mailto:merlinbotdri@microsoft.com).
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Migrate FabricBot Tasks to Config-as-Code" (#13832)

Issues.Title: Referencing netstandard 2.0 from WebSITE targeting .Net 4.8
Issues.Body:
I am having an issue referencing packages that target .NetStandard 2.0 from my webSITE (not application) project which targets .Net 4.8.

I see the following error:


> warning MSB3268: The primary reference "D:\a\1\s\iSAMS.Web.Controls\bin\Release\net48\iSAMS.Web.Controls.dll" could not be resolved because it has an indirect dependency on the framework assembly "netstandard, Version=2.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=cc7b13ffcd2ddd51" which could not be resolved in the currently targeted framework. ".NETFramework,Version=v4.8". To resolve this problem, either remove the reference "D:\a\1\s\iSAMS.Web.Controls\bin\Release\net48\iSAMS.Web.Controls.dll" or retarget your application to a framework version which contains "netstandard, Version=2.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=cc7b13ffcd2ddd51".


I have already added an assembly reference to the web.config as follows but it doesn't work:

```
<assemblies>
          <add assembly="netstandard, Version=2.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=cc7b13ffcd2ddd51" />
....
```

What am I doing wrong?
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Referencing netstandard 2.0 from WebSITE targeting .Net 4.8" (#1771)

Issues.Title: Value Tuple Could not load file or assembly 'System.ValueTuple, Version=4.0.1.0,
Issues.Body:
[tuple-not-loading-log.txt](https://github.com/dotnet/standard/files/5606091/tuple-not-loading-log.txt)

I have  web project which is targeted to 4.6.2 .net framework. This project is calling a framework library which is targeted to the same .net framework 4.6.2. But in the framework library project it is referencing the system.valuetuple of 4.3.0 via the nuget package. 
  <package id="System.ValueTuple" version="4.3.0" targetFramework="net461" />

This valuetuple 4.3.0 targets .net standard 1.0 and .net framework 4.5. But our UI project is targeting 4.6.2. And we are getting this valuetuple not able to load. Could you please tell me why so ? 

So if i am referencing a library that targets .net standard and .net framework of which the referencing project doesnt target to . How does the dll assemblies work here ? Really confused. Please help us

Basically I am getting this error. The detailed log is attached in tuple-not-loading file. 
_message: "ReflectionTypeLoadException: Could not load file or assembly 'System.ValueTuple, Version=4.0.1.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=cc7b13ffcd2ddd51' or one of its dependencies. The system cannot find the file specified."

 VS 2017 IDE is used to build the project
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Value Tuple Could not load file or assembly 'System.ValueTuple, Version=4.0.1.0, " (#1783)

Issues.Title: Assembly version for DispatchProxy shim is too low resulting in duplicate types for DispatchProxy
Issues.Body:
Create a new `netstandard2.1` library.
Add a reference to `<PackageReference Include="System.Reflection.DispatchProxy" Version="4.7.1" />`
Try to use `DispatchProxy`

Expected: 
`DispatchProxy` is resolved from `netstandard.dll`

Actual:
```
error CS0433: The type 'DispatchProxy' exists in both 'System.Reflection.DispatchProxy, Version=4.0.6.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a' and 'netstandard, Version=2.1.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=cc7b13ffcd2ddd51'
```


Build contains conflict message:
```
Encountered conflict between 'CopyLocal:C:\src\dotnet\runtime\artifacts\bin\testPackages\cache\system.reflection.dispatchproxy\4.7.1\lib\netstandard2.0\System.Reflection.DispatchProxy.dll' and 'Runtime:C:\Program Files\dotnet\packs\NETStandard.Library.Ref\2.1.0\ref\netstandard2.1\System.Reflection.DispatchProxy.dll'. Choosing 'CopyLocal:C:\src\dotnet\runtime\artifacts\bin\testPackages\cache\system.reflection.dispatchproxy\4.7.1\lib\netstandard2.0\System.Reflection.DispatchProxy.dll' because AssemblyVersion '4.0.6.0' is greater than '4.0.5.0'.
```

Other shims avoid this by setting the AssemblyVersion higher than that which was previously shipped.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Assembly version for DispatchProxy shim is too low resulting in duplicate types for DispatchProxy" (#1791)

Issues.Title: Update docs to reflect the status of .NET Standard in 2020
Issues.Body:
My understanding is, .NET Standard isn't the plan for standard .NET anymore. [The plan is now .NET 5.](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/the-future-of-net-standard/)

That isn't at all clear from the docs here which seem to be frozen in time. Suggest you make some slight edits to [faq.md](https://github.com/dotnet/standard/blob/master/docs/faq.md) and [README.md](https://github.com/dotnet/standard/blob/master/README.md) to reflect the new status of .NET Standard.

Issues.Title: Confusing behavior when using AssemblyInfo.cs
Issues.Body:
I'm completely perplexed.

I created a .Net Standard 2.0 library project.

I explicity added an AssemblyInfo.cs file, we need this because we use this utility to "stamp" our assemblies when we build them:

https://github.com/ygoe/NetRevisionTool

That utility is run as a pre/post build event and temporarily replaces placeholders in the AssemblyInformationalVersion attribute with runtime values (like repo folder name, commit ID, branch etc etc).

The completed assembly then has this info "burned" into it as we go forward.

.Net Standard is not working with this.

1. It seems to populatethe Package Version (in the project's Package section) with whatever we put into AssemblyInfo.cs for AssemblyInformationalVersion.
2. The version of the generated nuget package is always set as 1.0.0.

This is just mind blowing, is there no way we can do this seemingly simple operation any more if adopt .Net Standard?
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Confusing behavior when using AssemblyInfo.cs " (#1775)

Issues.Title: Questions about tagging within this repository / future versions
Issues.Body:
I was looking through this repository to determine if .NET Standard 2.1 libraries support default interface methods - it looks like they do, given the presence of `System.Runtime.CompilerServices.RuntimeFeature.DefaultImplementationsOfInterfaces`, as well as my own experimenting in VS 2019. It seems I was mistaken when thinking that this was not the case, so I'm glad this repository is easily accessible to determine this.

But while doing this, I found it difficult to determine what commits were included in this particular .NET Standard version. I see that there's a tag `v2.1.0-prerelease.19126.1`, as well as branches `release/2.0.0`, `release/3.0`, and `release/3.0-preview9`. One of the machines I tested VS 2019 on had a `netstandard.dll` assembly, itself targeting .NETStandard v2.1.0, with AssemblyInformationalVersion `1.1.0+a5b5f2e1e369972c8ff1e2183979fab6099f52ef`; this commit is part of the `release/3.0` branch. However, another machine had a same-named assembly, targeting .NETCoreApp v3.0 and AssemblyInformationalVersion `3.0.0+4ac4c0367003fe3973a3648eb0715ddb0e3bbcea`, and I don't see that commit hash in this repository.

Additionally, [this blog post](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/the-future-of-net-standard/) states that 2.1 will be the last version, but there is another prerelease tag `v2.2.0-prerelease.19564.1` and [on GitHub](https://github.com/dotnet/standard/releases/tag/v2.2.0-prerelease.19564.1) it's labelled .NET 5.0 Preview 2.

So I have a few questions for understanding this repository:

1. Since .NET Standard 2.1 is released, are there plans to continue development in this repository for e.g. the shared BCL going forward with .NET 5+?
2. Are there multiple kinds of `netstandard.dll` for a single .NET Standard version? E.g. are there separate versions for .NET Framework to reference and for .NET Core / .NET 5+ to reference?
3. Do the tags / branch names in this repository correspond to a .NET Standard version or something else?
4. It looks like there are patch releases; are there patch versions of a single .NET Standard version?
5. Are there other repositories which are being used to create `netstandard.dll`?
6. Is there a way to determine, from the repository, which commit is the one used for a given release?

Thank you for your consideration.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Questions about tagging within this repository / future versions" (#1777)

Issues.Title: Issue with resolving between .NET Standard 2.0 and .NET Standard 2.1
Issues.Body:
# Summary

.NET Standard version is not resolved correctly when a transitive dependency targets multiple .NET Standard versions.

# Environment

- Windows 10 (19042.746)
- .NET 5 SDK 5.0.201

# Minimal reproducible example
[bug.zip](https://github.com/dotnet/standard/files/6209239/bug.zip)

This is a three-project solution:
- `TestProject1` targeting `netcoreapp3.1`
- `ClassProject1` targeting `netstandard2.0`
- `ClassProject2` targeting *both* `netstandard2.0` and `netstandard2.1`

The project dependency chain is: `TestProject1 -> ClassProject1 -> ClassProject2`

A method (`ClassProject2Test1`) on `ClassProject2` is hidden behind a `#if NETSTANDARD2_0` preprocessor directive.

# The issue

The unit test failed with a `MissingMethodException`

```
[xUnit.net 00:00:00.38]     TestProject1.UnitTest1.Test1 [FAIL]
  Failed TestProject1.UnitTest1.Test1 [2 ms]
  Error Message:
   System.MissingMethodException : Method not found: 'Void ClassLibrary2.Class2.Class2Test1()'.
  Stack Trace:
     at ClassLibrary1.Class1.Class1Test1()
   at TestProject1.UnitTest1.Test1() in C:\Users\rex\source\netstandard-bug-repro\TestProject1\UnitTest1.cs:line 12
```

# Expected result

The compiler should have resolved the chain as:

`TestProject1 (.NET Core 3.1) -> ClassProject1 (.NET Standard 2.0) -> *ClassProject2 (.NET Standard 2.0)*` 

# Actual result

The compiler resolved the chain as:

`TestProject1 (.NET Core 3.1) -> ClassProject1 (.NET Standard 2.0) -> *ClassProject2 (.NET Standard 2.1)*` 

Which causes the exception because the offending method, `ClassProject2Test1`, was hidden by the `#if NETSTANDARD2_0` preprocessor directive.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Issue with resolving between .NET Standard 2.0 and .NET Standard 2.1" (#1787)

Issues.Title: [BUG] [UWP] GetManifestResourceInfo doesn't work on UWP
Issues.Body:
# Summary
I was trying to access Embedded resources on UWP. Something like this

```C#
manifestResourceInfo = typeof(App).Assembly.GetManifestResourceInfo("UwpEmbeddedResourceBug.appsettings.json");
```
However, this doesn't work on UWP, even though it works like a charm on iOS for instance. 
Further, when you try to use this in UWP debug mode, it will return non-null `manifestResourceInfo`, however internally the `FileName` property will be null. But if you try to use the same on Release build, instead you will get an exception

```
System.PlatformNotSupportedException: Operation is not supported on this platform.
   at System.Func`1.BeginInvoke(AsyncCallback, Object) + 0x1f
   at SharedLibrary!<BaseAddress>+0x9f17d7
   at UwpEmbeddedResourceBug.MainPage..ctor() + 0x3e
```

Issues.Title: Security Vulnerability due to System.Text.RegularExpressions
Issues.Body:
### Problem
There is a security vulnerability with the version of System.Text.RegularExpressions that is used by dotnet standard. The vulnerable version of System.Text.RegularExpressions is 4.3.0. Please see links below for the vulnerability documentation or discussion:

https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/en-US/vulnerability/CVE-2019-0820
https://github.com/dotnet/announcements/issues/111
https://cve.mitre.org/cgi-bin/cvename.cgi?name=CVE-2019-0820
https://snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-DOTNET-SYSTEMTEXTREGULAREXPRESSIONS-174708
https://www.whitesourcesoftware.com/vulnerability-database/CVE-2019-0820

The dotnet commit that fixed the vulnerability is https://github.com/dotnet/corefx/commit/19d4b1163d8a03dd90ff5fd1785eea6305112545

### Issue:
We use whitesource and it has caught this issue with high severity. We don't consume System.Text.RegularExpressions directly but packages that use this. Therefore as a transitive dependency due to NetStandard library, which is being used by several other dotnet packages like Castle Windsor, MassTransit etc., Once an update is available the others could followup and update.

### Code:

[The latest version 2.0.3 of Net standard ](https://www.nuget.org/packages/NETStandard.Library/) still seem to be using System.Text.RegularExpressions version 4.3.0 as per https://github.com/dotnet/standard/blob/master/src/apicompat/netstandard/netstandard.depproj

### Fix
Please update[ library](https://www.nuget.org/packages/NETStandard.Library/) targeting .netStandard 1.6 to update System.Text.RegularExpression package to latest secured version 4.3.1 to fix the security issue or kindly suggest alternatives.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Security Vulnerability due to System.Text.RegularExpressions" (#1786)
